title: Intermolecular Forces and Physical Properties
subtitle: Dispersion, dipoleâ€“dipole, and hydrogen bonding
subject: Chemistry
syllabusModule: Module 1: Properties and Structure of Matter
syllabusDotPoints: explore the similarities and differences between the nature of intermolecular and intramolecular bonds and the strength of the forces associated with each, in order to explain the:
inquiryQuestion: What binds atoms together in elements and compounds?
lessonIntent: Students can identify the IMFs in a molecule and use them to predict and explain boiling-point trends.
examSkill: Name all IMFs present (dispersion is always there), identify the strongest, and rank boiling points, accounting for the size effect.

introVoiceover.text: Welcome to HSC Science. This is HSC Chemistry. Today, intermolecular forces and physical properties. Let's get into it.

scenes.caption: Intermolecular Forces and Physical Properties (Year 11 Chemistry, Module 1, Lesson 10)

scenes.heading: Two near-twins, 160آ°C apart
scenes.body: Water and hydrogen sulfide have almost the same shape, yet water boils at 100آ°C and Hâ‚‚S at âˆ’60آ°C. Why the gulf?
scenes.callout: The forces between molecules, not inside them, decide this.
scenes.caption: Water and Hâ‚‚S look alike but boil 160آ°C apart.

voiceover.text: OK, last lesson we saw that water boils far above hydrogen sulfide, even though the two molecules look almost identical. Water boils at one hundred degrees, hydrogen sulfide at minus sixty, a gulf of one hundred and sixty degrees. Today we explain exactly why, by going right into the forces that act between molecules. These intermolecular forces decide boiling point, melting point, surface tension, and more, and being able to name them and rank them is one of the most testable skills in this whole module. So let's build the full toolkit.

scenes.heading: The three intermolecular forces
scenes.body: From weakest to strongest, three forces act between molecules, and dispersion is always present.

rows: H bonded to N, O, or F

bullets.text: Dispersion: temporary dipoles from fluctuating electron clouds â€” in every molecule.
Dipoleâ€“dipole: permanent partial charges in polar molecules.
Hydrogen bonding: needs H on N, O, or F, plus an adjacent lone pair.

scenes.secondary: Dispersion never switches off. A polar molecule has dispersion plus dipoleâ€“dipole; an H-bonder can have all three.
scenes.callout: Dispersion is always there; stronger forces stack on top.
scenes.caption: Dispersion < dipoleâ€“dipole < hydrogen bonding; dispersion is universal.

voiceover.text: There are three intermolecular forces, and you rank them weakest to strongest. First, dispersion forces, which come from temporary dipoles as the electron cloud constantly fluctuates. These are the weakest, but crucially they're present in every single molecule, polar or not. Second, dipole dipole forces, which come from the permanent partial charges in polar molecules. Third, and strongest, hydrogen bonding, which only forms when hydrogen is bonded to nitrogen, oxygen, or fluorine, with a lone pair on an adjacent molecule to accept it. Here's the key point students forget. Dispersion never switches off. A polar molecule has both dispersion and dipole dipole, and a hydrogen bonder can have all three acting at once.

scenes.heading: What controls dispersion strength

bullets.text: Number of electrons: more electrons â†’ more polarisable â†’ stronger dispersion.
That's why Iâ‚‚ > Brâ‚‚ > Clâ‚‚ > Fâ‚‚ in boiling point.
Molecular shape: straight chains contact along their full length.

scenes.callout: More electrons and more contact area mean stronger dispersion.
scenes.caption: Dispersion grows with electron count and with chain straightness.

voiceover.text: Since dispersion is in everything, it's worth knowing what makes it stronger. Two factors. First, the number of electrons. More electrons mean a larger, more easily distorted electron cloud, so stronger temporary dipoles and stronger dispersion. That's why boiling point climbs down the halogens, iodine above bromine above chlorine above fluorine, simply because they have more and more electrons. Second, molecular shape. A long, straight chain molecule can touch its neighbours along its entire length, giving more contact and stronger dispersion than a compact, branched version of the same formula. Take pentane. The straight chain, n-pentane, boils at thirty-six degrees, while its branched twin, neopentane, with the identical formula, boils at only nine and a half.

scenes.heading: The boiling-point prediction routine
scenes.body: Work through the molecule in a fixed order to find its strongest IMF, then rank.

bullets.text: Step 1: is H bonded directly to N, O, or F? â†’ hydrogen bonding.
Step 2: is the molecule polar? â†’ dipoleâ€“dipole (plus dispersion).
Step 3: otherwise â†’ dispersion only; compare electron count and shape.

scenes.secondary: Higher total IMF means higher boiling point. But a very large non-polar molecule can outrank a small hydrogen-bonded one.
scenes.callout: H-bonding? Polar? Size? In that order.
scenes.caption: Predict BP: check H-bonding, then polarity, then size.

voiceover.text: Here's the routine for any boiling point question, run it in a fixed order so you never miss a force. Step one, is hydrogen bonded directly to nitrogen, oxygen, or fluorine? If yes, hydrogen bonding is present, the strongest force. Step two, if not, is the molecule polar? If yes, it has dipole dipole forces on top of dispersion. Step three, if it's neither, then only dispersion acts, so you compare electron count and shape. Higher total intermolecular force means higher boiling point. But watch one subtlety. A very large non-polar molecule can actually outrank a small hydrogen bonded one, because its huge dispersion forces can dominate. So always weigh both the type of force and the size of the molecule.

scenes.heading: Rank three molecules
scenes.question: Rank by boiling point: propane (Câ‚ƒHâ‚ˆ, non-polar), chloromethane (CHâ‚ƒCl, polar), propan-1-ol (Câ‚ƒHâ‚‡OH, has Oâ€“H). Justify.
scenes.coachNote: Find the strongest IMF in each, then order them.
steps: Propane: no polar bonds, no N/O/Fâ€“H â†’ dispersion only
Chloromethane: polar Câ€“Cl â†’ dispersion + dipoleâ€“dipole
Propan-1-ol: Oâ€“H bond â†’ hydrogen bonding (strongest)
Order of strength: dispersion < dipoleâ€“dipole < hydrogen bonding
scenes.caption: Propane < chloromethane < propan-1-ol, by strongest IMF.

voiceover.text: Let's rank three molecules by boiling point. Propane, chloromethane, and propan-one-ol. Step one, propane has no polar bonds and no hydrogen on nitrogen, oxygen, or fluorine, so only dispersion forces act, the weakest. Step two, chloromethane has a polar carbon chlorine bond, so it has dipole dipole forces on top of dispersion, putting it in the middle. Step three, propan-one-ol has an oxygen hydrogen bond, so it can hydrogen bond, the strongest force. Step four, ranking the strongest force in each, dispersion is below dipole dipole, which is below hydrogen bonding. So the order from lowest to highest is propane at minus forty-two, chloromethane at minus twenty-four, then propan-one-ol way up at ninety-seven degrees. That jump to ninety-seven shows just how powerfully hydrogen bonding lifts a boiling point.

scenes.heading: The water anomaly
scenes.question: Group 16 hydrides: Hâ‚‚S âˆ’60آ°C, Hâ‚‚Se âˆ’41آ°C, Hâ‚‚Te âˆ’2آ°C â€” yet Hâ‚‚O boils at 100آ°C. Explain the anomaly.
scenes.coachNote: First find the normal trend, then the exception.
steps: Hâ‚‚S â†’ Hâ‚‚Se â†’ Hâ‚‚Te: BP rises with more electrons â†’ stronger dispersion
But Hâ‚‚O actually boils at 100آ°C â€” about 180آ°C higher
Oxygen is highly electronegative â†’ water forms strong Oâ€“Hآ·آ·آ·O hydrogen bonds
Answer: hydrogen bonding (absent in the others) lifts water's BP enormously âœ“
scenes.caption: Water breaks the Group 16 trend because it hydrogen-bonds.

voiceover.text: Now the famous water anomaly. Look at the Group sixteen hydrides going down. Hydrogen sulfide boils at minus sixty, hydrogen selenide at minus forty-one, hydrogen telluride at minus two. That's a smooth rise, because each one has more electrons and therefore stronger dispersion forces. Step one, that's the normal trend. Step two, if you extrapolate it backwards, water should boil around minus eighty. Step three, but water actually boils at one hundred, roughly one hundred and eighty degrees higher than predicted, a massive anomaly. Step four, the cause is hydrogen bonding. Oxygen is highly electronegative, so water forms strong oxygen to oxygen hydrogen bonds, while sulfur, selenium, and tellurium aren't electronegative enough to do the same. So hydrogen bonding, present only in water, lifts its boiling point far above the trend.

scenes.heading: Three IMF traps
scenes.body: Trap 1: thinking HCl forms hydrogen bonds because it's polar. Chlorine isn't electronegative enough â€” HCl has only dispersion plus dipoleâ€“dipole.
scenes.secondary: Trap 2: forgetting dispersion in polar molecules. It acts between every pair of molecules. Trap 3: assuming hydrogen bonding always wins. A much larger non-polar molecule (e.g. hexadecane) can boil higher than water.
scenes.callout: HCl doesn't H-bond; dispersion is always there; size can beat H-bonding.
scenes.caption: HCl can't H-bond; never forget dispersion; size can override H-bonding.

voiceover.text: Three traps. Trap one, thinking hydrogen chloride forms hydrogen bonds just because it's polar. It doesn't. Hydrogen bonding needs hydrogen attached to nitrogen, oxygen, or fluorine, and chlorine isn't electronegative enough, so hydrogen chloride has only dispersion and dipole dipole forces. Trap two, forgetting dispersion in polar molecules. Students write that water only has hydrogen bonds, but dispersion acts between every pair of molecules, so always list it too. Trap three, assuming hydrogen bonding always wins. It usually does for similar sized molecules, but a much larger non-polar molecule, like the long chain hexadecane, can actually boil higher than water, because its enormous dispersion forces outweigh water's hydrogen bonding. So always weigh size against force type.

scenes.heading: Quick check
scenes.question: Predict the BP order, lowest to highest: CHâ‚„, CHâ‚ƒF, CHâ‚ƒOH. Justify each.
answerSteps: CHâ‚„: non-polar, no N/O/Fâ€“H â†’ dispersion only (weakest)
CHâ‚ƒF: polar Câ€“F, but H is on C not F â†’ dispersion + dipoleâ€“dipole
CHâ‚ƒOH: Oâ€“H bond â†’ hydrogen bonding (strongest)
Rank by strongest IMF in each
scenes.caption: CHâ‚„ < CHâ‚ƒF < CHâ‚ƒOH, set by their strongest IMF.

voiceover.text: Quick check. Predict the boiling point order, lowest to highest, for methane, fluoromethane, and methanol. Pause and work it through. Right, let's check it. Step one, methane is non-polar with no hydrogen on nitrogen, oxygen, or fluorine, so only dispersion forces act, the weakest. Step two, fluoromethane has a polar carbon fluorine bond, but watch out, the hydrogen is on carbon, not on fluorine, so there's no hydrogen bonding, just dispersion plus dipole dipole. Step three, methanol has an oxygen hydrogen bond, so it hydrogen bonds, the strongest force. Step four, ranking by strongest force gives methane lowest, then fluoromethane, then methanol. So the order is methane at minus one hundred and sixty-two, fluoromethane at minus seventy-eight, and methanol at sixty-five. The trap was assuming fluoromethane hydrogen bonds, but the hydrogen sits on carbon, so it can't.

scenes.heading: Lock it in
points: Three IMFs: dispersion < dipoleâ€“dipole < hydrogen bonding.
Dispersion is in every molecule and grows with electrons and contact area.
Hydrogen bonding needs H on N, O, or F â€” HCl doesn't qualify.
Predict BP: check H-bonding, then polarity, then size.
A very large non-polar molecule can outrank a small H-bonded one.
scenes.finalPrompt: Comparing BPs? Name every IMF, then rank by the strongest.
scenes.caption: Five takeaways: three IMFs, dispersion always present, H-bonding rules, the size effect.

voiceover.text: Let's lock it in. Five things to take away. First, there are three intermolecular forces, dispersion, then dipole dipole, then hydrogen bonding, weakest to strongest. Second, dispersion is present in every molecule, and it grows with the number of electrons and the contact area. Third, hydrogen bonding only forms when hydrogen is on nitrogen, oxygen, or fluorine, so hydrogen chloride doesn't qualify. Fourth, predict a boiling point by checking hydrogen bonding first, then polarity, then size. And fifth, a very large non-polar molecule can outrank a small hydrogen bonded one, because dispersion can dominate. That completes the bonding inquiry. Great work through Module one. See you next lesson.
